narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, an LSAT score of 28, solid Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 7 in your school, and a middle-class family income background, along with planning to attend full-time. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 48% chance that you would fail it on your first try—high enough that it tipped the decision against admitting you, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which is notably below the typical 36.7 average and signaled to the model a higher risk of struggling on the bar. Counterbalancing that somewhat were your Year 3 decile ranking and Year 1 decile ranking, both at 7—stronger than the averages of about 5.5 and 5.7—showing the model that your school performance leaned toward passing potential. These academic deciles were the next most important factors, pulling the prediction in a more favorable direction compared to many applicants. Your full-time status, common among 93% of students, and middle-income bracket didn't sway things much either way. Overall, while your in-school grades stood out positively against typical students, the LSAT's strong negative pull dominated, making your profile riskier than the safer averages the model favors for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many from similar backgrounds overcome early hurdles—but the model weighs these metrics conservatively. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, as that single change could flip predictions for schools using similar tools. Strengthening your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or highlighting leadership in Hispanic communities might also help in appeals or other applications. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and explore prep programs tailored for first-generation or underrepresented students to build bar readiness. With targeted steps, you can reshape how models—and schools—see your path to passing the bar and practicing law.
